## DEEP SYMBOLIC REGRESSION: RECOVERING MATHEMATICAL EXPRESSIONS FROM - DATA VIA RISK SEEKING POLICY GRADIENTS

**Brenden K. Petersen** _[∗]_ Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory Livermore, CA, USA bp@llnl.gov

## ABSTRACT

Discovering the underlying mathematical expressions describing a dataset is a core challenge for artificial intelligence. This is the problem of _symbolic regression_ . Despite recent advances in training neural networks to solve complex tasks, deep learning approaches to symbolic regression are underexplored. We propose a framework that leverages deep learning for symbolic regression via a simple idea: use a large model to search the space of small models. Specifically, we use a recurrent neural network to emit a distribution over tractable mathematical expressions and employ a novel risk-seeking policy gradient to train the network to generate better-fitting expressions. Our algorithm outperforms several baseline methods (including Eureqa, the gold standard for symbolic regression) in its ability to exactly recover symbolic expressions on a series of benchmark problems, both with and without added noise. More broadly, our contributions include a framework that can be applied to optimize hierarchical, variable-length objects under a blackbox performance metric, with the ability to incorporate constraints in situ, and a risk-seeking policy gradient formulation that optimizes for best-case performance instead of expected performance.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Understanding the mathematical relationships among variables in a physical system is an integral component of the scientific process. Symbolic regression aims to identify these relationships by searching over the space of tractable (i.e. concise, closed-form) mathematical expressions to best fit a dataset. Specifically, given a dataset ( _X, y_ ), where each point _Xi ∈_ R _[n]_ and _yi ∈_ R, symbolic regression aims to identify a function _f_ : R _[n] →_ R that best fits the dataset, where the functional form of _f_ is a short mathematical expression. The resulting expression can be readily interpreted and/or provide useful scientific insights simply by inspection. In contrast, conventional regression imposes a single model structure that is fixed during training, often chosen to be expressive (e.g. a neural network) at the expense of being easily interpretable.

> _∗_ Corresponding author.

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Symbolic regression exhibits several unique features that make it an excellent test problem for benchmarking automated machine learning (AutoML) and program synthesis methods: (1) there exist well-established, challenging benchmark problems with stringent success criteria (White et al., 2013); (2) there exist well-established baseline methods (most notably, the Eureqa algorithm (Schmidt & Lipson, 2009)); and (3) the reward function is computationally expedient, allowing sufficient experiment replicates to achieve statistical significance. Most other AutoML tasks, e.g. neural architecture search (NAS), do not exhibit these features; in fact, even simply _evaluating_ the efficiency of the discrete search itself is a known challenge within NAS (Yu et al., 2019).

The space of mathematical expressions is discrete (in model structure) and continuous (in model parameters), growing exponentially with the length of the expression, rendering symbolic regression a challenging machine learning problem—thought to be NP-hard (Lu et al., 2016). Given this large, combinatorial search space, traditional approaches to symbolic regression typically utilize evolutionary algorithms, especially genetic programming (GP) (Koza, 1992; Schmidt & Lipson, 2009; Back et al., 2018).¨ In GP-based symbolic regression, a population of mathematical expressions is “evolved” using evolutionary operations like selection, crossover, and mutation to improve a fitness function. While GP can be effective, it is also known to scale poorly to larger problems and to exhibit high sensitivity to hyperparameters.

Deep learning has permeated almost all areas of artificial intelligence, from computer vision (Krizhevsky et al., 2012) to optimal control (Mnih et al., 2015). However, deep learning may seem incongruous with or even antithetical toward symbolic regression, given that neural networks are typically highly complex, difficult to interpret, and rely on gradient information. We propose a framework that resolves this incongruity by tying deep learning and symbolic regression together with a simple idea: use a large model (i.e. neural network) to search the space of small models (i.e. symbolic expressions). This framework leverages the representational capacity of neural networks to generate interpretable expressions, while entirely bypassing the need to interpret the network itself.

We present _deep symbolic regression_ (DSR), a gradient-based approach for symbolic regression based on reinforcement learning. In DSR, a recurrent neural network (RNN) emits a distribution over mathematical expressions. Expressions are sampled from the distribution, instantiated, and evaluated based on their fitness to the dataset. This fitness is used as the reward signal to train the RNN using a novel risk-seeking policy gradient algorithm. As training proceeds, the RNN adjusts the likelihood of an expression relative to its reward, assigning higher probabilities to better expressions.

We demonstrate that DSR outperforms several baseline methods, including two commercial software algorithms. We summarize our contributions as follows: (1) a novel method for symbolic regression that outperforms several baselines on a set of benchmark problems, (2) an autoregressive generative modeling framework for optimizing hierarchical, variable-length objects that accommodates in situ constraints, and (3) a novel risk-seeking policy gradient objective and accompanying Monte Carlo estimation procedure that optimizes for best-case performance instead of average performance.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Deep learning for symbolic regression.** Several recent approaches leverage deep learning for symbolic regression. AI Feynman (Udrescu & Tegmark, 2020) propose a problem-simplification tool for symbolic regression. They use neural networks to identify simplifying properties in a dataset (e.g. multiplicative separability, translational symmetry), which they exploit to recursively define simplified sub-problems that can then be tackled using any symbolic regression algorithm. In GrammarVAE, Kusner et al. (2017) develop a generative model for discrete objects that adhere to a pre-specified grammar, then optimize them in latent space. They demonstrate this can be used for symbolic regression; however, the method struggles to exactly recover expressions, and the generated expressions are not always syntactically valid. Sahoo et al. (2018) develop a symbolic regression framework using neural networks whose activation functions are symbolic operators. While this approach enables an end-to-end differentiable system, backpropagation through activation functions like division or logarithm requires the authors to make several simplifications to the search space, ultimately precluding learning certain simple classes of expressions like _[√] x_ or sin( _x/y_ ). We address and/or directly compare to these works in Appendices C and E.

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**AutoML and program synthesis.** Symbolic regression is related to both automated machine learning (AutoML) and program synthesis, in that they all involve a search for an executable program (i.e. expression) to solve a particular task (i.e. to fit data) (Abolafia et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2017; Riedel et al., 2016). More specifically, our framework has many parallels to a body of works within AutoML that use an autoregressive RNN to define a distribution over discrete objects and use reinforcement learning to optimize this distribution under a black-box performance metric (Zoph & Le, 2017; Ramachandran et al., 2017; Bello et al., 2017; Abolafia et al., 2018). For example, in neural architecture search (Zoph & Le, 2017), an RNN searches the space of neural network architectures, encoded by a sequence of discrete “tokens” specifying architectural properties (e.g. number of neurons) of each layer. The length of the sequence is fixed or scheduled during training; in contrast, our framework defines a search space that is both inherently hierarchical and variable length. Ramachandran et al. (2017) search the space of neural network activation functions. While this space is hierarchical in nature, the authors (rightfully) constrain it substantially by positing a functional unit that is repeated sequentially, thus restricting their search space back to a fixed-length sequence. However, a repeating-unit constraint is not practical for symbolic regression because the ground truth expression may have arbitrary structure.

**Autoregressive models.** The RNN-based distribution over expressions used in DSR is autoregressive, meaning each token is conditioned on the previously sampled tokens. Autoregressive models have proven to be useful for audio and image data (Oord et al., 2016a;b) in addition to the AutoML works discussed above; we further demonstrate their efficacy for hierarchical expressions. GraphRNN defines a distribution over graphs that generates an adjacency matrix one column at a time in autoregressive fashion (You et al., 2018). In principle, GraphRNN could be constrained to define a distribution over expressions, since trees are a special case of graphs. However, GraphRNN constructs graphs breadth-first, whereas expressions are more naturally represented using depth-first traversals (Li et al., 2005). Further, DSR exploits the hierarchical nature of trees by providing the parent and sibling as inputs to the RNN, and leverages the additional structure of expression trees that a node’s value determines its number of children (e.g. cosine is a unary operator and thus has one child).

**Risk-aware reinforcement learning.** Many of the AutoML methods discussed above suffer from what we call the “expectation problem.” That is, policy gradient methods are fundamentally suited for optimizing _expectations_ ; however, domains like neural architecture search and symbolic regression are evaluated by the few or single best-performing samples. Thus, there is a mismatch between the training objective function and the true desired objective: to maximize best-case performance. Abolafia et al. (2018) address the expectation problem by maintaining a priority queue of the best seen samples and using supervised learning to increase the likelihood of those top samples. Similarly, Liang et al. (2018) use a memory buffer to augment a policy gradient with off-policy training. These methods, however, only apply in the context of reinforcement learning environments with both deterministic transition dynamics and deterministic rewards. In contrast, the _risk-seeking policy gradient_ introduced here is general, applying to any reinforcement learning environment and any stochastic policy gradient algorithm trained using batches, e.g. on Atari using proximal policy optimization (Schulman et al., 2017). Lastly, our risk-seeking policy gradient is closely related to the EPOpt- _ε_ algorithm used for robust reinforcement learning (Rajeswaran et al., 2016), which is based on a risk-averse policy gradient formulation (Tamar et al., 2014).

## 3 METHODS

Our overall approach involves representing mathematical expressions as sequences, developing an autoregressive model to generate expressions under a pre-specified set of constraints, and developing a risk-seeking policy gradient to train the model to generate better-fitting expressions.

## 3.1 GENERATING EXPRESSIONS WITH A RECURRENT NEURAL NETWORK

We leverage the fact that mathematical expressions can be represented using _symbolic expression trees_ . Expression trees are a type of binary tree in which internal nodes are mathematical operators and terminal nodes are input variables or constants. Operators may be unary (i.e. one argument, such as sine) or binary (i.e. two arguments, such as multiply). Further, we can represent an expression tree as a sequence of node values or “tokens” by using its pre-order traversal (i.e. by visiting each node depth-first, then left-to-right). This allows us to generate an expression tree sequentially while still

**Providing hierarchical inputs to the RNN.** Conventionally, the input to the RNN when sampling a token would be a representation of the previously sampled token. However, the search space for symbolic regression is inherently hierarchical, and the previously sampled token may actually be very distant from the next token to be sampled in the expression tree. To better capture hierarchical information, we provide as inputs to the RNN a representation of the parent and sibling nodes of the token being sampled. We introduce an empty token for cases in which a node does not have a parent or sibling. Pseudocode for identifying the parent and sibling nodes given a partial traversal is provided in Subroutine 1 in Appendix A.

**Constraining the search space.** Under our framework, it is straightforward to apply a priori constraints to reduce the search space. To demonstrate, we impose several simple, domain-agnostic constraints: (1) Expressions are limited to a pre-specified minimum and maximum length. We selected minimum length of 4 to prevent trivial expressions and a maximum length of 30 to ensure expressions are tractable. (2) The children of an operator should not all be constants, as the result would simply be a different constant. (3) The child of a unary operator should not be the inverse of that operator, e.g. log(exp( _x_ )) is not allowed. (4) Descendants of trigonometric operators should not be trigonometric operators, e.g. sin( _x_ + cos( _x_ )) is not allowed because cosine is a descendant of sine. While still semantically meaningful, such composed trigonometric operators do not appear in virtually any scientific domain.

We apply these constraints in situ (i.e. concurrently with autoregressive sampling) by zeroing out the probabilities of selecting tokens that would violate a constraint. Pseudocode for this process is provided in Subroutine 2 in Appendix A. This process ensures that samples always adhere to all constraints, without rejecting samples post hoc. In contrast, imposing constraints with GP requires rejecting evolutionary operations post hoc (Fortin et al., 2012), which can be problematic (Craenen et al., 2001), and as we show in our experiments, can actually reduce performance.

**Reward function.** Once a pre-order traversal is sampled, we instantiate the corresponding symbolic expression and evaluate it with a reward function. A standard fitness measure in GP-based symbolic regression is normalized root-mean-square error (NRMSE), the root-mean-square error normalized by the standard deviation of the target values, _σy_ . That is, given a dataset ( _X, y_ ) of size _n_ and candidate

**Constant optimization.** If the library _L_ includes the constant token, sampled expressions may include several constant placeholders. These can be viewed as parameters _ξ_ of the symbolic expression, which we optimize by maximizing the reward function: _ξ[⋆]_ = arg max _ξ R_ ( _τ_ ; _ξ_ ), using a nonlinear optimization algorithm, e.g. BFGS (Fletcher, 2013). We perform this inner optimization loop for each sampled expression as part of the reward computation before performing each training step.

## 3.2 TRAINING THE RNN USING POLICY GRADIENTS

The reward function is not differentiable with respect to _θ_ ; thus, we turn to reinforcement learning to train the RNN to produce better-fitting expressions. In this view, the distribution over mathematical expressions _p_ ( _τ |θ_ ) is like a policy, sampled tokens are like actions, the parent and sibling inputs are like observations, sequences corresponding to expressions are like episodes, and the reward is a terminal, undiscounted reward only computed when an expression completes.

**Standard policy gradient.** We first consider the standard policy gradient objective to maximize _J_ std( _θ_ ), defined as the expectation of a reward function _R_ ( _τ_ ) under expressions from the policy: _J_ std( _θ_ ) = _[.]_ E _τ ∼p_ ( _τ |θ_ ) [ _R_ ( _τ_ )]. The standard REINFORCE policy gradient (Williams, 1992) can be used to maximize this expectation via gradient ascent:

This result allows one to estimate the expectation using samples from the distribution. Specifically, an unbiased estimate of _∇θJ_ std( _θ_ ) can be obtained by computing the sample mean over a batch of _N_ sampled expressions _T_ = _{τ_[(] _[i]_[)] _}[N] i_ =1[:]

This is an unbiased gradient estimate, but in practice it has high variance. To reduce variance, it is common to subtract a baseline function _b_ from the reward. As long as the baseline is not a function of

the current batch of expressions, the gradient estimate is still unbiased. Common choices of baseline functions are a moving average of rewards or an estimate of the value function. Intuitively, the gradient step increases the likelihood of expressions above the baseline and decreases the likelihood of expressions below.

**Risk-seeking policy gradient.** The standard policy gradient objective, _J_ std( _θ_ ), is defined as an _expectation_ . This is the desired objective for control problems in which one seeks to optimize the average performance of a policy. However, in domains like symbolic regression, program synthesis, or neural architecture search, the final performance is measured by the single or few best-performing samples found during training. Similarly, one might be interested in a policy that achieves a “high score” in a control environment (e.g. Atari). For such problems, _J_ std( _θ_ ) is not an appropriate objective, as there is a mismatch between the objective being optimized and the final performance metric; this is the “expectation problem.” To address this disconnect, we propose an alternative objective that focuses learning only on _maximizing best-case performance_ . We first define _Rε_ ( _θ_ ) as the (1 _− ε_ )-quantile of the distribution of rewards under the current policy. Note this is a function of _θ_ but is typically intractable. We then propose a new learning objective, _J_ risk( _θ_ ; _ε_ ), parameterized by _ε_ :

_J_ risk( _θ_ ; _ε_ ) = _[.]_ E _τ ∼p_ ( _τ |θ_ ) [ _R_ ( _τ_ ) _| R_ ( _τ_ ) _≥ Rε_ ( _θ_ )] (1)

This objective aims to increase the reward of the top _ε_ fraction of samples from the distribution, without regard for samples below that threshold. This objective bears close resemblance with _ε_ - conditional value at risk (CVaR), for which the “ _≤_ ” symbol is used instead of “ _≥_ ” and the _ε_ -quantile of rewards is used instead of the (1 _− ε_ )-quantile. Optimizing CVaR is a form of risk-averse learning that results in a policy that is robust against catastrophic outcomes (Tamar et al., 2014; Rajeswaran et al., 2016). In contrast, optimizing _J_ risk( _θ_ ; _ε_ ) yields a _risk-seeking_ policy gradient that aims to increase best-case performance at the expense of lower worst-case and average performances. Essentially, this is the standard REINFORCE Monte Carlo estimate with two differences: (1) theory suggests a specific baseline, _Rε_ ( _θ_ ), whereas the baseline for standard policy gradients is non-specific, chosen by the user; and (2) effectively, only the top _ε_ fraction of samples from each batch are used in the gradient computation. This process is essentially the opposite of the approach used to optimize CVaR (Tamar et al., 2014; Rajeswaran et al., 2016) for risk-averse reinforcement learning, in which only the _bottom ε_ fraction of samples from each batch are used.

Note that in Proposition 1 and the corresponding Monte Carlo estimation procedure, _τ_ need not refer to a symbolic expression or even a discrete object. For example, it may refer to a state-action trajectory in a control problem. Thus, the risk-seeking policy gradient formulation is general and can easily be applied to any reinforcement learning environment using any stochastic policy gradient algorithm trained using batches, e.g. proximal policy optimization (Schulman et al., 2017).

Lastly, in accordance with the maximum entropy reinforcement learning framework (Haarnoja et al., 2018), we provide a bonus to the loss function proportional to the entropy of the sampled expressions. Pseudocode for DSR is shown in Algorithm 1. Source code is made available at https://github.com/brendenpetersen/deep-symbolic-regression.

regression community (White et al., 2013). Each benchmark is defined by a ground truth expression, a training and test dataset, and a set of allowed operators, described in Table 2 in Appendix D. The training data is used to compute the reward for each candidate expression, the test data is used to evaluate the best found candidate expression at the end of training, and the ground truth expression is used to determine whether the best found candidate expression was correctly recovered.

We compared DSR against five strong symbolic regression baselines: (1) **PQT** : an implementation of our framework trained using priority queue training (Abolafia et al., 2018) in place of the risk-seeking policy gradient, (2) **VPG** : a “vanilla” implementation of our framework using the standard policy gradient (with baseline) in place of the risk-seeking policy gradient, (3) **GP** : a standard GP-based symbolic regression implementation (Fortin et al., 2012), (4) **Eureqa** : popular commercial software[3] based on Schmidt & Lipson (2009), the gold standard for symbolic regression, and (5) **Wolfram** : commercial software[4] based on Markov chain Monte Carlo and nonlinear regression (Fortuna, 2015). Each baseline is further detailed in Appendix C. Notably, the two RNN-based baselines (PQT and VPG) differ from DSR only via their training objective, i.e. a supervised objective for PQT and the standard policy gradient for VPG. All other aspects of our framework (in situ constraints, hierarchical RNN inputs, constant optimization) are also included for these baselines. While GP can incorporate post hoc constraints, we found that doing so actually hinders performance (see Appendix F); thus, they are excluded for GP we exclude all constraints other than the maximum length constraint.

Each experiment consists of 2 million expression evaluations for DSR, PQT, VPG, and GP, by which point training curves have levelled off. Hyperparameters were tuned on benchmarks Nguyen-7 and Nguyen-10 using a grid search comprising 800 hyperparameter combinations for GP and 81 combinations for each of DSR, PQT, and VPG (see Appendix D for details). Commercial software algorithms (Eureqa and Wolfram) do not expose hyperparameters and were run until completion. All experiments were replicated with 100 different random seeds for each benchmark expression. Wolfram results are only presented for one-dimensional benchmarks because the method is not applicable to higher dimensions. Additional experiment details are provided in Appendix D. Training curves are provided in Appendix F.

In Table 1, we report the recovery rate for each benchmark. We use the strictest definition of recovery: exact symbolic equivalence, as determined using a computer algebra system, e.g. SymPy (Meurer et al., 2017). In Table 9 in Appendix F, we report recovery on several additional variants of Nguyen benchmarks in which we introduced real-valued constants (to demonstrate the constant optimizer) or altered the functional form to make the problems more challenging. DSR significantly outperforms all five baselines in its ability to exactly recover benchmark expressions. **Characterizing the risk-seeking policy gradient.** The intuition behind the risk-seeking policy gradient is that it explicitly optimizes for best-case performance, possibly at the expense of average performance. We demonstrate this visually in Figure 2 by comparing the empirical reward distributions when trained with either the risk-seeking or standard policy gradient for Nguyen-8. (Analogous plots for all Nguyen benchmarks are provided in Appendix F.) Interestingly, at the end of training, the mean reward over the full batch (an estimate of _J_ std( _θ_ )) is larger when training with the standard policy gradient, even though the risk-seeking policy gradient produces larger mean over the top _ε_ fraction of the batch (an estimate of _J_ risk( _θ_ ; _ε_ )) and a superior best expression. This is consistent with the intuition of maximizing best-case performance at the expense of average performance. In contrast, the best-case performance of the standard policy gradient plateaus early in training (Figure 2E, dashed blue curve), whereas the risk-seeking policy gradient continues to increase until the end of training (Figure 2E, dashed black curve).

**Ablation studies.** Algorithm 1 includes several additional components relative to a “vanilla” policy gradient search. We performed a series of ablation studies to quantify the effect of each of these components, along with the effects of the various constraints on the search space. In Figure 3, we show recovery rate for DSR on the Nguyen benchmarks for each ablation. While no single ablation leads to catastrophic failure, combinations of ablations can cause large degradation in performance.

**Noisy data and amount of data.** We evaluated the robustness of DSR to noisy data by adding independent Gaussian noise to the dependent variable, with mean zero and standard deviation proportional to the root-mean-square of the dependent variable in the training data. In Figure 4, we varied the proportionality constant from 0 (noiseless) to 10 _[−]_[1] and evaluated each algorithm (except Wolfram, which catastrophically fails for even the smallest noise level) across all Nguyen benchmarks. Because expressions can overfit to the noise in these experiments, we defined recovery as exact symbolic equivalence on any expression along the reward-complexity Pareto front at the end of training (see Appendix D for details). Increasing the dataset size may help prevent overfitting by smoothing the reward function. Thus, we repeated the noise experiments with 10-fold larger training datasets (Figure 4, dashed lines). For each dataset size, DSR consistently outperforms all baselines.

**Performance under reward noise.** The risk-seeking policy gradient is derived from a conditional expectation, and is thus well-justified for tasks with stochastic rewards. In contrast, PQT assumes deterministic rewards. Since symbolic regression is a deterministic task, we emulated a stochastic reward function by adding independent Gaussian noise directly to the reward function: _R[′]_ ( _τ_ ) = _R_ ( _τ_ ) + _N_ (0 _, σ_ ). In Figure 5, we compare DSR and PQT performance under increasing reward noise on benchmark Nguyen-4, a task that is an easier exploitation problem but difficult exploration problem. As reward noise increases, recovery for PQT heavily relies on exploration and luck, whereas DSR recovery remains stable even for high reward noise.

## 5 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK

We introduce a reinforcement learning approach to symbolic regression that outperforms state-of-theart baselines in its ability to recover exact expressions on benchmark tasks. Since both DSR and GP generate expression trees, there are many opportunities for hybrid methods, for example including several generations of evolutionary operations within the inner optimization loop. Our framework includes a flexible distribution over hierarchical, variable-length objects that allows imposing in situ constraints. Our framework is easily extensible to other domains, which we save for future work; for example, searching the space of expressions to be used as control policies in reinforcement learning environments, or searching the space of organic molecular structures for high binding affinity to a reference compound. Our risk-seeking policy gradient formulation can also be applied to more traditional reinforcement learning domains; for example, optimizing for a high score (instead of average score) in Atari video games.

comments.title: Fundamental mismatch between stated risk-seeking objective and implemented gradient estimator
comments.explanation: The paper defines $ J_{\text{risk}}(\theta; \varepsilon) $ as the conditional expectation $ \mathbb{E}[R(\tau) \mid R(\tau) \geq R_\varepsilon(\theta)] $, but the Monte Carlo gradient estimator implemented in Algorithm 1 corresponds to the gradient of the *unnormalized* tail expectation $ \mathbb{E}[R(\tau) \cdot \mathbf{1}_{R(\tau) \geq R_\varepsilon(\theta)}] $, not the conditional expectation. The correct gradient of a conditional expectation must account for both the expectation and the changing probability mass $ P(R(\tau) \geq R_\varepsilon(\theta)) $, which depends on $ \theta $. The actual estimator — summing $ (R(\tau^{(i)}) - R_\varepsilon) \nabla_\theta \log p(\tau^{(i)} | \theta) $ over top-performing samples — omits correction terms for the quantile's dependence on $ \theta $, resulting in a biased gradient. While this estimator is practical and widely used (e.g., in evolution strategies), the theoretical framing misrepresents the objective and creates a critical inconsistency between the stated formulation and the method. This undermines the paper's theoretical contribution and may mislead readers about the soundness of the derivation.

comments.quote: To bound the reward function, we apply a squashing function: _R_ ( _τ_ ) = 1 _/_ (1 + NRMSE).

However, the text says 'squashing function' — which typically maps to a bounded interval, often [0,1] or [-1,1]. But note that in the objective J_risk(θ; ε) = E[R(τ) | R(τ) ≥ R_ε(θ)], higher rewards are better. However, NRMSE increases with worse fit, so R(τ) = 1/(1+NRMSE) is decreasing in error — good. The issue arises when considering extremal values: if NRMSE = 0, R = 1; if NRMSE → ∞, R → 0. So reward is bounded in (0,1]. However, in reinforcement learning, it's common to have non-negative rewards, so this is acceptable. The potential concern is whether the risk-seeking objective is maximizing the right tail of a reward that is bounded above by 1 but can approach zero. But this is not an error. The actual issue comes from the fact that the authors use R(τ) in expectation and conditional expectation, but do not clarify whether this reward is ever transformed further (e.g. centered or normalized). However, the expression is mathematically correct and consistent. The initial concern resolves with context, so no real issue remains. Flagging this only to confirm resolution: it's not an error.

comments.title: Ambiguity in NRMSE formula due to OCR artifacts
comments.quote: NRMSE = _σ_ 1 _y_  _n_ 1  _ni_ =1[(] _[y][i][ −][f]_[(] _[X][i]_[))][2][.][To bound the reward function, we apply a] squashing function: _R_ ( _τ_ ) = 1 _/_ (1 + NRMSE).
comments.explanation: At first glance, the formula for NRMSE appears malformed: 'σ 1 y  n 1  ni =1' is clearly corrupted by OCR. Based on standard definitions and context (NRMSE is root-mean-square error normalized by σ_y), the intended expression is almost certainly NRMSE = (1/σ_y) * sqrt((1/n) Σ_{i=1}^n (y_i - f(X_i))^2). The paper correctly identifies NRMSE as a standard metric in symbolic regression, and the use of a squashing function R(τ) = 1 / (1 + NRMSE) is reasonable to map errors to (0,1]. However, the current rendering is unreadable and could mislead a reader not familiar with the metric. Since the full expression is syntactically broken and not recoverable without external knowledge, this constitutes a meaningful ambiguity, though the intended meaning is reconstructable given domain expertise.

comments.title: Inconsistent handling of expression tree uniqueness in pre-order traversal
comments.quote: > 2Given an expression tree, the corresponding mathematical expression is unique; however, given an expression, its expression tree is not unique. For example, _x_[2] and _x × x_ are equivalent expressions but yield different trees. For simplicity, we use _τ_ somewhat abusively to refer to an expression where it technically refers to an expression tree (or equivalently, its pre-order traversal).
comments.explanation: The authors correctly acknowledge that expression trees are not in bijective correspondence with mathematical expressions—different trees can represent the same function. They state they abuse notation by using τ to refer to an expression when it technically refers to a tree or traversal. This is acceptable and common. However, this creates a potential inconsistency with the likelihood definition: p(τ | θ) = ∏ ψ^{(i)}_{L(τ_i)}, which assigns a probability to a traversal τ. If two traversals (e.g. for x^2 vs x*x) are semantically equivalent but structurally different, they will receive different probabilities under the model, even though they should arguably be treated as the same candidate solution. The authors do not address how this affects reward computation or model training. While handling equivalence classes of expressions is a known hard problem, the paper does not mention this subtlety when discussing reward maximization or recovery accuracy, which could mislead readers into thinking exact structural match is necessary for 'exact recovery' in experiments. This weakens the validity of the claimed exact recovery comparisons.

comments.title: Missing derivative for risk-seeking objective due to truncated text
comments.quote: We then propose a new learning objective, _J_ risk( _θ_ ; _ε_ ), parameterized by _ε_ :
comments.explanation: The passage cuts off after introducing the risk-seeking objective J_risk(θ; ε) as the conditional expectation of reward above the (1−ε)-quantile. However, the full definition of the objective and, critically, its gradient estimator (which is central to the method) are not included in the provided text. While the abstract and related work mention a Monte Carlo estimation procedure, the actual derivation or form of the risk-seeking policy gradient update is missing here. Given that this is a 'novel' contribution, the omission leaves the method underspecified in this section. However, since this is a forward reference (the full derivation may appear later in the paper), and given that risk-seeking variants like CVaR-based policy gradients are established, this is likely just an artifact of the passage boundary. Thus, no issue is flagged.

comments.title: Misleading example of hierarchical input in Figure 1
comments.quote: For example, the fifth and sixth tokens sampled in Figure 1 are adjacent nodes in the traversal but are four edges apart in the expression tree.
comments.explanation: The example aims to justify using parent/sibling inputs instead of just the previous token. In Figure 1, token 5 is 'x' and token 6 is 'log', which are indeed adjacent in traversal. However, in the expression tree (sin(const * x) / log(y)), 'x' is the right child of '*', which is the right child of 'sin', which is the left operand of '/', while 'log' is the right operand of '/'. Thus, the path goes up two levels to the root and down two levels — totaling four edges. This is accurate. The RNN uses the parent and sibling of the current node to be sampled: for token 6 ('log'), the parent is '/' and there is no sibling (since '/' has two children: sin(...) and log(y), and 'sin' is the left child, so 'log' has no left sibling). The use of hierarchical inputs is sound and well-motivated. No actual issue here.

comments.title: Underspecified constraint application during autoregressive sampling
comments.quote: We apply these constraints in situ (i.e. concurrently with autoregressive sampling) by zeroing out the probabilities of selecting tokens that would violate a constraint.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify a key advantage of their method: hard constraints can be enforced during sampling by modifying the token distribution. However, the passage does not specify *when* or *how* these constraints are evaluated. For instance, constraint (2): 'The children of an operator should not all be constants' — this can only be checked once all children are generated. But in pre-order traversal, the operator is visited before its children. Thus, at the time of sampling its children, the model could prevent constant-only children. But what if one child is a variable and the other is to be a constant? The model must anticipate future choices. The description is too vague about whether constraints are enforced eagerly or require lookahead. While pseudocode exists in Appendix A, the main text should clarify whether the constraints are local (enforceable at sampling time) or require future information. This underspecification could affect reproducibility, especially for constraint (4) about trigonometric descendants, which requires tracking ancestry depth.

comments.title: Risk-seeking objective mischaracterized as conditional expectation
comments.explanation:
At first glance, the definition of $ J_{\text{risk}}(\theta; \varepsilon) $ as a conditional expectation — the expected reward given that $ R(\tau) \geq R_\varepsilon(\theta) $ — appears consistent with the stated goal of improving top-performing expressions. However, examining the Monte Carlo gradient estimator and the algorithm (specifically line 9 of Algorithm 1), the actual computation uses $ (R(\tau^{(i)}) - R_\varepsilon(\theta)) \nabla_\theta \log p(\tau^{(i)} | \theta) $ for $ R(\tau^{(i)}) \geq R_\varepsilon(\theta) $, which aligns with a *truncated risk-seeking policy gradient*, not a gradient of the conditional expectation.

The issue arises because the true gradient of the conditional expectation would involve additional terms due to the dependence of the threshold $ R_\varepsilon(\theta) $ on $ \theta $ (via the distribution $ p(\tau | \theta) $). The derivation in Appendix B likely follows Tamar et al. (2014) for CVaR, which accounts for such dependence via a policy gradient that includes both a truncated expectation and a term involving the derivative of the quantile. But the Monte Carlo estimator proposed here — which uses only the top $ \varepsilon $-fraction of rewards and subtracts $ R_\varepsilon(\theta) $ — corresponds to an approximation that neglects the quantile's gradient. This results in a biased gradient estimate unless $ R_\varepsilon(\theta) $ is treated as fixed (e.g., using a moving estimate).

In the algorithm, this is pragmatically handled by using the batch empirical quantile (line 6), and the gradient is computed relative to that fixed threshold. But the notation $ J_{\text{risk}}(\theta; \varepsilon) $ as a conditional expectation suggests a more theoretically grounded objective than what is actually optimized. A more accurate objective would be an expectation weighted by an indicator (like $ \mathbb{E}_\tau [ R(\tau) \cdot \mathbf{1}_{R(\tau) \geq R_\varepsilon(\theta)} ] $), which matches the estimator.

The text acknowledges $ R_\varepsilon(\theta) $ is a function of $ \theta $, so the dependence cannot be ignored. Without proper handling (e.g., through baseline subtraction in the gradient or detached quantile computation), this introduces bias. The field generally accepts empirical quantiles in such settings (e.g., in QR-DQN), but the formal objective should reflect the estimator used. There is a mismatch between the stated $ J_{\text{risk}} $ and the actual implementation.

Thus, while the algorithm is likely effective in practice and similar to known risk-aware methods, the theoretical framing oversimplifies the gradient’s dependence on $ \theta $ through the threshold.

comments.title: Ambiguous specification of constant optimization procedure
comments.quote: We perform this inner optimization loop for each sampled expression as part of the reward computation before performing each training step.
comments.explanation:
The passage states that constant optimization occurs 'for each sampled expression' and 'before performing each training step'. This raises confusion: is constant optimization performed on every sampled expression in each batch, or only on the best ones? Algorithm 1 (line 4) unambiguously shows that constant optimization is applied to every expression in the batch before reward computation, which is correct and consistent.

However, the text could mislead a reader into thinking that constant optimization happens only at higher levels of training (e.g., on elites only), especially since only rewards above the quantile are used in the gradient. The statement doesn't clarify whether poor-fitting expressions still undergo constant optimization, which is computationally expensive.

In context, the method is well-specified in the algorithm, so this is not a methodological gap. But the prose description omits key operational details — such as warm-starting from prior estimates, convergence criteria for BFGS, or whether optimization is attempted even for syntactically invalid expressions (though constraints prevent most). In Appendix A or D, these may be clarified, but as presented, a reader might not realize that every expression, regardless of structure or potential fit, undergoes full nonlinear optimization.

This is standard in symbolic regression (e.g., in Eureqa), so the assumption is reasonable. Still, given the computational cost, underspecifying convergence tolerance, max iterations, or initialization strategy risks reproducibility.

comments.title: Incorrect description of Monte Carlo gradient estimate in text
comments.quote: This result suggests a simple Monte Carlo estimate of the gradient from a batch of _N_ samples: > where _R_[˜] _ε_ ( _θ_ ) is the empirical (1 _− ε_ )-quantile of the batch of rewards, and **1** _x_ returns 1 if condition _x_ is true and 0 otherwise.
comments.explanation:
The text refers to a formula right after this line (which is missing in the provided passage but presumably Equation (2) in the full paper), stating that the Monte Carlo estimate uses the indicator function and the empirical quantile.

From Algorithm 1, line 9, we see the actual gradient estimate is: $ \hat{g}_1 = \text{ReduceMean}((R - R_\varepsilon) \nabla_\theta \log p(\tau | \theta)) $, but only over expressions with $ R(\tau) \geq R_\varepsilon $. This matches
\[
\nabla_\theta J \approx \frac{1}{N_\text{high}} \sum_{i: R(\tau^{(i)}) \geq \tilde{R}_\varepsilon} \left(R(\tau^{(i)}) - \tilde{R}_\varepsilon\right) \nabla_\theta \log p(\tau^{(i)} | \theta)
\]
which is a truncated, baseline-subtracted REINFORCE estimator.

However, the text says the estimate comes from Proposition 1, which claims to give the gradient of $ J_{\text{risk}} $. But this estimator is not the gradient of the conditional expectation $ \mathbb{E}[R(\tau) \mid R(\tau) \geq R_\varepsilon(\theta)] $, because:

1. It subtracts $ R_\varepsilon $, making it resemble a baseline-modified gradient rather than a pure conditional expectation.
2. It uses the empirical quantile $ \tilde{R}_\varepsilon $, treated as constant.
3. It does not include correction terms for the fact that $ R_\varepsilon(\theta) $ depends on $ \theta $, which would be required in a true policy gradient derivation.

The derivation in Tamar et al. (2014) for CVaR includes a correction term for the quantile’s gradient. If the current paper follows that adaptation (as claimed), then Proposition 1 must include such a term. But the estimator in the algorithm and the text description does not, suggesting the theoretical derivation is simplified or misaligned with practice.

Thus, while the estimator is practical and commonly used (e.g., in evolution strategies), the link between Proposition 1 and the Monte Carlo estimate is underspecified. The text implies the estimate is exact or unbiased given the objective, but without quantile-gradient correction, it is biased.

comments.title: Misleading claim about generality of risk-seeking framework
comments.quote: Note that in Proposition 1 and the corresponding Monte Carlo estimation procedure, _τ_ need not refer to a symbolic expression or even a discrete object. For example, it may refer to a state-action trajectory in a control problem. Thus, the risk-seeking policy gradient formulation is general and can easily be applied to any reinforcement learning environment using any stochastic policy gradient algorithm trained using batches, e.g. proximal policy optimization (Schulman et al., 2017).
comments.explanation:
The authors correctly point out that the risk-seeking objective doesn’t depend on the nature of $ \tau $, and the estimator can, in principle, be applied in continuous or high-dimensional control settings.

However, the nontrivial dependence of $ R_\varepsilon(\theta) $ on $ \theta $, as discussed earlier, becomes a more serious issue in complex RL environments. In PPO or other advanced policy gradient methods, simply truncating gradients to the top performers and subtracting the empirical quantile—without accounting for how the policy affects the threshold—is known to cause instability or bias.

Standard risk-aware RL methods like EPOpt (Rajeswaran et al., 2016) carefully handle this through robust optimization or trajectory sampling under uncertainty, not just batch quantile truncation. The claim of generality is technically true — one *can* plug this estimator into any batched policy gradient algorithm — but it risks being ineffective or unstable without additional stabilization mechanisms (e.g., target networks for the threshold, moving quantiles, or implicit differentiation).

The authors are right that the idea is modular and simple, but the statement that it 'can easily be applied' overstates its plug-and-play viability, especially compared to more mature risk-averse or risk-seeking approaches in RL.

In symbolic regression, the reward is deterministic and the search space discrete, so quantile estimation is stable. In Atari or robotics, rewards are stochastic and trajectories are high-dimensional, so the empirical quantile can fluctuate wildly, harming learning. This domain difference is not acknowledged.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of $ \tilde{R}_\varepsilon(\theta) $ affects gradient estimator correctness
comments.quote: Monte Carlo estimate: $ \widehat{\nabla_\theta J} \approx \frac{1}{|\mathcal{T}|} \sum_{\tau^{(i)} \in \mathcal{T}} (R(\tau^{(i)}) - \tilde{R}_\varepsilon(\theta)) \nabla_\theta \log p(\tau^{(i)} | \theta) $, where $ \mathcal{T} = \{ \tau^{(i)} : R(\tau^{(i)}) \geq \tilde{R}_\varepsilon(\theta) \} $
comments.explanation: At first glance, this estimator appears to be a standard risk-seeking gradient, but the use of $ \tilde{R}_\varepsilon(\theta) $ as both threshold and baseline raises concern. The empirical quantile $ \tilde{R}_\varepsilon(\theta) $ is computed from the same batch used for the gradient, so using it in both the thresholding set $ \mathcal{T} $ and in the deviation term $ R(\tau^{(i)}) - \tilde{R}_\varepsilon(\theta) $ introduces explicit dependence of the baseline on the sample batch. This violates the standard condition that baselines must be independent of the sampled trajectories to preserve unbiasedness. The paper's assumptions note that 'the baseline $ b $ in policy gradient is independent of the current batch of samples to preserve unbiasedness,' yet here the baseline $ \tilde{R}_\varepsilon(\theta) $ is clearly batch-dependent. While the risk-seeking gradient in Proposition 1 uses the true $ R_\varepsilon(\theta) $, the Monte Carlo estimate replaces it with the empirical $ \tilde{R}_\varepsilon(\theta) $, which is inherently stochastic and correlated with the samples used in the sum. This leads to a biased gradient estimator, and while such estimators are used in practice (e.g., in PPO), the lack of acknowledgment or discussion of bias is problematic. The authors may intend for this to be an approximation, but the derivation implies exactness via Proposition 1, and this inconsistency between theory and practice is not addressed. This matters because the entire justification for the method relies on the unbiasedness and theoretical grounding of the gradient update.

comments.title: Mismatch between prose and formal objective in risk-seeking definition
comments.quote:
We then propose a new learning objective, $ J_{\text{risk}}(\theta; \varepsilon) $, parameterized by $ \varepsilon $:

    [section 3] $ J_{\text{risk}}(\theta; \varepsilon) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim p(\tau | \theta)} [ R(\tau) \mid R(\tau) \geq R_\varepsilon(\theta)] $ (1)
comments.explanation: The text defines $ J_{\text{risk}}(\theta; \varepsilon) $ as a conditional expectation: the expected reward given that the reward is at least the $ (1-\varepsilon) $-quantile. However, in the key equation for the gradient (Proposition 1), the objective is implicitly treated as $ \mathbb{E}[ R(\tau) \cdot \mathbf{1}(R(\tau) \geq R_\varepsilon(\theta))] $, not the conditional expectation. The correct gradient of the conditional expectation would involve additional terms due to the normalization by $ P(R(\tau) \geq R_\varepsilon(\theta)) $, which depends on $ \theta $. The derivation in Proposition 1 matches the gradient of the unnormalized top-$ \varepsilon $ tail expectation, not the normalized conditional expectation. The Monte Carlo estimator further supports this, as it sums over the top fraction without dividing by the proportion. Thus, there is a fundamental inconsistency: the prose defines a conditional expectation, but the math and algorithm implement the unnormalized tail expectation. This is a critical conflation because optimizing the conditional expectation (average of top $ \varepsilon $) vs. the tail expectation (sum of top $ \varepsilon $) leads to different inductive biases. The authors likely intend the tail expectation (as in CVaR), but stating it as a conditional expectation misrepresents the objective and undermines theoretical clarity.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Nguyen-8 expression and recovery rate
comments.explanation: The benchmark expression for Nguyen-8 is presented as crossed out '√' followed by _x_, which appears to be a corrupted OCR rendering. Given the naming convention of the Nguyen benchmarks (Nguyen-1 to Nguyen-7 are polynomials and trig expressions), and standard knowledge of the suite, Nguyen-8 is typically $ \sqrt{x} $ or $ x^{0.5} $. The recovery rate for DSR is reported as 96%, while Eureqa achieves 71% and GP only 5%. This seems plausible, but the notation '~~_√_~~ _x_' is ambiguous—it could suggest $ \sqrt{x} $, or it might be interpreted as $ \sqrt{\cdot} \circ x $, or even a negated expression. However, from context (Table 1 and Figure 3), DSR performs well on most benchmarks, and 96% recovery for a square root is reasonable. The issue is not the value but the clarity of the benchmark definition. The cross-out may be an OCR error where '√' was rendered poorly and post-processed as ~~√~~, but in any case, the intended expression should be unambiguously stated. This is a minor issue because experts familiar with the benchmark will recognize it, but a careful reader unfamiliar with Nguyen-8 might misinterpret the target. The paper should clearly define all benchmark expressions in clean LaTeX.

comments.title: Missing normalization in NRMSE definition affects reward scaling
comments.quote: NRMSE = $ \frac{1}{\sigma_y} \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - f(X_i))^2} $
comments.explanation: The definition of NRMSE matches standard usage in symbolic regression, and the reward $ R(\tau) = 1 / (1 + \text{NRMSE}) $ is consistent with it. However, the reward function's behavior depends crucially on whether NRMSE is correctly normalized. The definition uses $ \sigma_y $, the standard deviation of the target values, which is the correct normalization to make NRMSE dimensionless and comparable across datasets. This is standard and appropriate. No issue here upon inspection—this is correctly formulated. Initial concern about potential missing square root or incorrect denominator was resolved by confirming with standard definitions. The reward squashing via $ 1/(1+\text{NRMSE}) $ ensures boundedness between 0 and 1, which is good for stability. So while the reward depends on correct computation of $ \sigma_y $, the formula itself is correct. This is a case where the authors got it right.

comments.title: Ambiguity in ablation study labels in Figure 3
comments.quote:
No risk/ent. 40% 50%
No risk/con.
No ent./con. 20% DSR VPG 25% DSR
comments.explanation: The labels in Figure 3, such as 'No risk/ent.' and 'No risk/con.', are ambiguous. Do they mean 'no risk-seeking and no entropy' or 'no risk-seeking or no entropy'? Given the context and the trend in performance degradation, it is likely that 'No risk/ent.' means removing both risk-seeking and entropy components. The figure shows a clear drop when multiple components are removed, supporting this interpretation. However, the slash notation is unconventional and could be misread as a choice (e.g., one or the other removed). Standard notation would use '+' or 'and', or list them separately. This could mislead a careful reader into thinking these are alternative ablations rather than joint removals. The text says 'combinations of ablations can cause large degradation', which confirms that multiple components are removed together. So context resolves the ambiguity, but the labeling is still poor practice. This is a minor issue because the overall trend is clear and the caption helps, but it could be improved.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and well-motivated framework for addressing symbolic regression through the lens of reinforcement learning, successfully bridging the gap between high-capacity neural search engines and the demand for interpretable, parsimonious output. The introduction is exemplary in its clarity, effectively situating the work within the broader context of AutoML and justifying the use of recurrent neural networks as a search strategy for discrete structures. The authors’ shift from standard policy gradients to a "risk-seeking" formulation is a significant methodological contribution, logically addressing the unique requirements of finding high-performance outliers in a search space rather than optimizing for the expected mean. While the foundational logic is sound, the paper’s ultimate success will likely depend upon the technical rigor of the appendices and the robustness of the empirical comparisons against non-deep-learning baselines; specifically, the transition between the theoretical motivation and the concrete implementation must ensure that the "risk-seeking" gradient does not introduce instabilities or convergence bottlenecks that are common in sophisticated reinforcement learning configurations.

comments.title: Recurring OCR errors in mathematical notation
comments.quote: Multiple instances, e.g., 'R_[n]' in paragraph 7, NRMSE formula in paragraph 31, and likelihood product in paragraph 22.
comments.explanation: The paper contains extensive OCR corruption in mathematical expressions, including garbled summation symbols, misindexed parameters, and dimension notation (e.g., R_[n] instead of R^n). These errors make key methodology and reward metrics difficult to parse or implement independently.

comments.title: Variable notation overloading
comments.quote: T = {\tau^{(i)} }_{i=1}^N versus |\tau| = T
comments.explanation: The symbol 'T' is used both to represent the total length of a traversal (|τ| = T) and the batch of sampled expressions. This ambiguity creates confusion in the provided pseudocode and gradient estimator formulas.

comments.title: Theoretical gaps in Risk-Seeking gradient derivation
comments.quote: Formula for the risk-seeking gradient estimate involving R_ε(θ) in paragraph 39.
comments.explanation: The authors define a gradient for the risk-seeking objective using a sample-dependent threshold (R_ε). They treat this as an unbiased gradient without addressing the bias introduced by the dependence of the quantile threshold on the parameters θ, or the necessary derivative terms required by the Leibniz rule when the integration bounds depend on θ. This claim of unbiasedness is theoretically problematic for a risk-seeking objective.

comments.title: Logical ambiguity in 'exact symbolic equivalence' definition
comments.quote: we defined recovery as exact symbolic equivalence on any expression... at the end of training
comments.explanation: The authors define recovery via 'exact symbolic equivalence' even for noisy benchmarks. Since noisy benchmarks obscure the ground truth, it is logically impossible to verify 'exact' equivalence to a hidden noiseless ground truth without clarification of the experimental setup (e.g., whether they have access to the noiseless function for validation). This phrasing conflates fitting performance with the ability to recover the specific ground truth.

comments.title: Inconsistent descriptions of constraint enforcement
comments.quote: This process ensures that samples always adhere to all constraints, without rejecting samples post hoc.
comments.explanation: The text claims constraints are applied 'in situ' to avoid post hoc rejection, yet the implementation uses masking (zeroing probabilities). While common, the authors represent masking as distinct from rejection despite the underlying operations being functionally similar, leading to ambiguity regarding the sampler's true behavior.

comments.title: Vanishing gradient concerns with reward squashing
comments.quote: R ( τ ) = 1 / (1 + NRMSE)
comments.explanation: The reward function mapping NRMSE to (0, 1] can lead to extreme saturation (vanishing gradients) when models perform poorly early in training. The paper lacks a discussion of how this design choice impacts the stability of reinforcement learning for symbolic regression tasks.

The introduction is exemplary in its clarity, effectively situating the work within the broader context of AutoML and justifying the use of recurrent neural networks as a search strategy for discrete structures. The authors’ shift from standard policy gradients to a "risk-seeking" formulation is a significant methodological contribution, logically addressing the unique requirements of finding high-performance outliers in a search space rather than optimizing for the expected mean. While the foundational logic is sound, the paper’s ultimate success will likely depend upon the technical rigor of the appendices and the robustness of the empirical comparisons against non-deep-learning baselines; specifically, the transition between the theoretical motivation and the concrete implementation must ensure that the "risk-seeking" gradient does not introduce instabilities or convergence bottlenecks that are common in sophisticated reinforcement learning configurations.

comments.title: OCR error in dataset definition
comments.quote: given a dataset ( _X, y_ ), where each point _Xi ∈_ R _[n]_ and _yi ∈_ R
comments.explanation: The text states that each point 'X_i' is in R^[n]. Mathematically, if X is a dataset of points, it is standard for each individual sample 'X_i' to be a vector in R^n. The notation 'R_[n]' is almost certainly an OCR misread of 'R^n'. While context makes the intent clear, this is a recurring notation error in the provided text that could confuse a reader regarding the dimensionality of the input space.

comments.title: Ambiguous variable notation for NRMSE
comments.explanation: The OCR rendering 'σ_ 1 _y_' and the summation symbols are corrupted. While it is clear from the context of symbolic regression and the definition of NRMSE that the authors intend '1/σ_y * sqrt(1/n * sum((y_i - f(X_i))^2))', the formula as printed is mathematically nonsensical due to the formatting of indices and Greek characters. This makes the primary reward metric, which is central to the paper's methodology, difficult to parse without external knowledge of the formula.

comments.title: Ambiguous indexing in likelihood product
comments.explanation: The product index notation '[|] i[τ]_ =1' uses a symbol 'i' that seems to be a misread (likely an OCR artifact from an intended 'i=1'). Furthermore, the expression uses |τ| as the upper limit of the product, which is correct for the length of the traversal, but the indexing within the product (τi) combined with the potentially malformed OCR tokens makes it momentarily confusing to verify if this is consistent with standard autoregressive probability definitions. While the intent is clearly the product of conditional probabilities, the notation is OCR-degraded which may mislead a reader performing an independent implementation.

comments.title: Potential contradiction in constraint application description
comments.quote: This process ensures that samples always adhere to all constraints, without rejecting samples post hoc.
comments.explanation: The text states that constraints are applied 'in situ' to avoid post hoc rejection, yet the previous sentence describes 'zeroing out the probabilities of selecting tokens that would violate a constraint.' While 'in situ' modification of the categorical distribution is a standard technique for autoregressive models to ensure validity, it is technically a form of 'rejection' (or masking) of the probability mass that would lead to invalid tokens. This is a common phrasing, but it could be framed more precisely to avoid ambiguity regarding whether invalid paths are explicitly sampled then checked vs. masked during sampling.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in the reward squashing function
comments.quote: NRMSE = σ 1 σy ∑ n 1 ∑ ni =1(( yi - f( Xi ))2 ).
comments.explanation: Looking at the definition provided for NRMSE, there is an evident OCR-related notation issue. The text includes two summation symbols ('∑ ∑') and an orphaned 'n 1' (likely intended to be 1/n). Conventionally, NRMSE is defined as the RMSE divided by the standard deviation of the targets. Here, it appears the authors intended: NRMSE = (1/(σy)) * sqrt((1/n) * ∑_{i=1}^{n} (yi - f(Xi))^2). The current representation is mathematically garbled due to the OCR process, making it difficult to verify if the squaring or root operations are properly accounted for in the provided text snippet.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of the Risk-seeking objective function
comments.quote: J risk( θ ; ε ) = . E τ ∼p ( τ |θ ) [ R ( τ ) | R ( τ ) ≥ Rε ( θ )]
comments.explanation: The formula provided for J_risk seems to imply a conditional expectation: E[R(τ) | R(τ) ≥ R_ε(θ)]. However, standard RL literature for 'risk-seeking' gradients usually involves optimizing the objective directly over the tail distribution. While the notation '|' is likely intended to denote the subset condition (the top ε fraction), it could be misread by a reader as a standard conditional probability expectation, which would yield a value independent of the fraction ε itself without proper scaling. The authors establish this as an exploration of the top ε, but the notation is slightly non-standard and should be clarified to avoid confusion with conditional expectations.

comments.title: Gradient estimate notation inconsistency
comments.quote: Specifically, an unbiased estimate of $\nabla_\theta J_{\text{std}}(\theta)$ can be obtained by computing the sample mean over a batch of $N$ sampled expressions $T = \{\tau^{(i)} \}_{i=1}^N$
comments.explanation: In the previous paragraph, the authors define $J_{\text{std}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim p(\tau|\theta)} [R(\tau)]$. The standard REINFORCE gradient estimate is $\hat{g} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \nabla_\theta \log p(\tau^{(i)}|\theta) (R(\tau^{(i)}) - b)$. However, the text here introduces the notation $T = \{\tau^{(i)} \}_{i=1}^N$ to represent the batch, but in section 3.2, the gradient estimate for $J_{\text{std}}$ is described without providing the explicit estimator formula (the provided text cuts off or refers to an equation not shown). More importantly, the variable $T$ is already defined as the length of the traversal ($|\tau| = T$) earlier in the paper. Overloading $T$ as both the total length of an expression and the set of sampled expressions creates significant potential for confusion, especially when reading the subsequent Algorithm 1 where $T$ is used as the batch.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of risk-seeking gradient update
comments.quote: $\hat{g}_1 \leftarrow \text{ReduceMean}((R - R_\epsilon) \nabla_\theta \log p(T |\theta))$
comments.explanation: In step 9 of Algorithm 1, the gradient is computed using $\log p(T |\theta)$. Based on the preceding text and the definition of the likelihood of an expression, $T$ refers to the batch of expressions (likely a typo for a specific $\tau^{(i)}$ or the set of expressions). However, the expression $\log p(T|\theta)$ is syntactically ambiguous. It implies the log-likelihood of the set $T$, yet the gradient estimate for the risk-seeking policy objective (Proposition 1) involves a summation over indices of expressions that meet the threshold condition. While context suggests this is a vectorized implementation of the estimator in Proposition 1, the formal representation $p(T|\theta)$ is notationally inconsistent with the previously established $p(\tau|\theta)$.

comments.title: Inconsistent formulation of risk-seeking gradient update
comments.quote: ∇θ J_risk(θ; ε) ≈ (1/N) Σ_{i=1}^N [ 1_{R(τ_i) ≥ R_ε(θ)} * (R(τ_i) - R_ε(θ)) * ∇θ log p(τ_i|θ) ]
comments.explanation: In the summary and earlier discussions, the risk-seeking gradient is defined as the gradient of the expectation of rewards in the top epsilon fraction. The provided formula uses the (1-ε)-quantile R_ε(θ) as the baseline. However, in the standard interpretation of policy gradients (REINFORCE), the baseline is typically a scalar or function subtracted from the reward to reduce variance, not a threshold that filters the sample set. The formula shows an indicator function 1_{R(τ_i) ≥ R_ε(θ)} which correctly identifies the top fraction, but it then multiplies by (R(τ_i) - R_ε(θ)). If the expectation is taken over the full distribution and only the top samples contribute, this is mathematically sound as a conditional expectation gradient. However, it is slightly ambiguous whether the sum should be normalized by N or by the number of samples exceeding the threshold (N*ε). While context implies the standard Monte Carlo estimate over i=1 to N, it is technically an estimate of the gradient of an expectation over a truncated distribution, and the normalization factor relative to the empirical quantile makes this expression potentially inconsistent if interpreted as a pure sample mean.

comments.title: Unclear handling of negative values in reward squashing
comments.quote: R ( τ ) = 1 / (1 + NRMSE)
comments.explanation: The reward function is defined as 1 / (1 + NRMSE). Since NRMSE is defined as (1/σ_y) * sqrt((1/n) * Σ(y_i - f(X_i))^2), NRMSE is non-negative by definition. However, if the expression f(X_i) performs very poorly, NRMSE can be quite large, leading to a reward close to 0. The authors claim this 'bounds' the reward. While this is mathematically true (R ∈ (0, 1]), relying on this specific squashing function can lead to extremely small gradients when NRMSE is large. The paper does not discuss potential vanishing gradient issues for poor models in the early stages of training, which is a common concern in RL for symbolic regression.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'exact symbolic equivalence' definition for noisy benchmarks
comments.quote: we defined recovery as exact symbolic equivalence on any expression along the reward-complexity Pareto front at the end of training
comments.explanation: The authors state that for noisy benchmarks, they recover expressions by checking 'exact symbolic equivalence'. It is unclear how they determine equivalence when the target expression is hidden behind noise. Usually, equivalence is checked via algebraic simplification or numerical sampling (symbolic regression software packages like SymPy do this). Given that these benchmarks have 'added noise', it is logically impossible to define 'exact symbolic equivalence' to the original noiseless ground truth unless the model successfully ignores the noise perfectly. This phrasing is slightly misleading as it conflates 'best fit on noisy data' with 'recovery of ground truth'.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Risk-Seeking Gradient Definition
comments.quote: ∇θ J_risk(θ; ε) ≈ (1/N) Σ_{i=1}^N [ 1_{R(τ_i) ≥ R_ε(θ)} * (R(τ_i) - R_ε(θ)) * ∇θ log p(τ_i|θ) ]
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the passage defines the risk-seeking gradient estimate. While the logic follows the standard policy gradient (REINFORCE) with a baseline, the mathematical derivation of the gradient of the conditional expectation E[R | R ≥ R_ε] is non-trivial. Simply subtracting the quantile R_ε(θ) from the reward inside the expectation is a valid baseline approach for a fixed distribution, but when the quantile R_ε itself depends on θ, the derivative of the quantile term with respect to θ typically introduces an additional term (based on the Leibniz rule/gradient of the integral). The authors treat only the indicator and the R(τ_i) - R_ε part, ignoring the potential bias introduced by the dependence of the threshold R_ε on the parameters θ. While this is common in heuristic RL implementations, claiming it is an unbiased estimate of the gradient of the objective J_risk without addressing the derivative of the threshold parameter is a significant theoretical gap.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding the baseline in the gradient estimate
comments.quote: ∇θJ_std(θ) ≈ (1/N) Σ [∇θ log p(τ_i|θ) * (R(τ_i) - b)]
comments.explanation: The text states that 'as long as the baseline is not a function of the current batch of expressions, the gradient estimate is still unbiased.' However, it defines the risk-seeking baseline implicitly as R_ε(θ). Since R_ε(θ) is calculated empirically from the batch of samples {τ_i} in the iteration, it is indeed a function of the current batch. Using a sample-dependent baseline can introduce bias if not treated carefully. While this is a recognized challenge in RL, the claim of the estimate being 'unbiased' (as stated in the summary of the standard gradient) is technically compromised when applied to the risk-seeking case, and the text does not acknowledge the risk of bias when the baseline is computed on the current batch.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents Deep Symbolic Regression (DSR), a novel framework that leverages recurrent neural networks trained with a risk-seeking policy gradient to recover mathematical expressions, effectively bridging deep learning and symbolic regression. The work demonstrates high quality and significance, tackling a notoriously difficult combinatorial optimization problem with a methodology that claims to surpass established baselines like Eureqa; the formulation of a risk-seeking objective to optimize best-case performance rather than expected return is a theoretically interesting contribution with potential broader applicability. The writing is largely clear and well-motivated, providing a solid intuition for why neural policies can outperform genetic programming in this domain. However, the clarity is marred by a prominent typo in the title ('FROM - DATA') and non-standard notation 'R[n]' for the input domain, which detract from the presentation's professionalism. Additionally, the reviewer should ensure the full paper rigorously justifies the computational trade-offs of training a generative policy versus sampling-based evolutionary methods, and explicitly details how the risk-seeking mechanism interacts with noisy datasets to prevent over-optimization of spurious high-fitness solutions.

comments.title: Inconsistent tree definition (binary vs. variable arity)
comments.quote: Expression trees are a type of binary tree in which internal nodes are mathematical operators and terminal nodes are input variables or constants. Operators may be unary (i.e. one argument, such as sine) or binary (i.e. two arguments, such as multiply).
comments.explanation: The authors define expression trees as a 'type of binary tree', which strictly implies every internal node has exactly two children. However, they immediately state that operators can be unary, meaning they only require one child. This is a technical contradiction unless dummy children are used, which is never mentioned. Later text notes that 'a node’s value determines its number of children', aligning with variable-arity trees. While common in GP literature, this definitional looseness should be corrected for rigorous readers or implementers.

comments.title: Missing critical algorithmic and proposition formulas (PDF extraction artifact)
comments.quote: Specifically, an unbiased estimate of ∇θJ std(θ ) can be obtained by computing the sample mean over a batch of N sampled expressions T = { τ_[( i )] _}[N] i =1[:] This is an unbiased gradient estimate, but in practice it has high variance.
comments.explanation: Multiple key mathematical expressions are entirely absent, indicating a PDF-to-text extraction failure. This includes the standard REINFORCE gradient estimator (para 35), the full gradient derivation in Proposition 1 (para 41), and the corresponding Monte Carlo estimator. Without these explicit formulas, the mathematical form of the estimators is underspecified, breaking logical derivations and preventing precise method reproduction. This is a critical gap for the methodology section.

comments.title: Theoretical inconsistency in risk-seeking baseline formulation and application
comments.quote: Essentially, this is the standard REINFORCE Monte Carlo estimate with two differences: (1) theory suggests a specific baseline, Rε ( θ_ ), whereas the baseline for standard policy gradients is non-specific, chosen by the user; and (2) effectively, only the top ε fraction of samples from each batch are used in the gradient computation.
comments.explanation: The authors claim their risk-seeking objective theoretically dictates a specific baseline $R_\epsilon(\theta)$, but this claim conflicts with both standard REINFORCE theory and their own implementation. First, the stated condition for baseline unbiasedness incorrectly restricts baselines from depending on the 'current batch,' whereas the strict requirement is independence from the sampled trajectory $\tau$. Second, and more critically, the algorithm (Alg 1, line 9) subtracts $R_\epsilon(\theta)$ only from the top-$\varepsilon$ filtered samples. Mathematically, the gradient of the conditional expectation $\mathbb{E}[R \mid R \ge R_\epsilon]$ does not involve subtracting the threshold. Subtracting it only inside the indicator function changes the optimization target to $\mathbb{E}[(R-R_\epsilon)\mathbb{I}(R \ge R_\epsilon)]$ and violates the baseline validity condition, creating a direct disconnect between the stated objective and the implemented update rule.

comments.title: Ignored bias from continuous constant optimization (bilevel structure)
comments.quote: _ξ[⋆]_ = arg max _ξ_ _R_ ( _τ_ ; _ξ_ ), using a nonlinear optimization algorithm, e.g. BFGS (Fletcher, 2013). We perform this inner optimization loop for each sampled expression as part of the reward computation before performing each training step.
comments.explanation: Treating constant placeholders as continuous parameters optimized per sampled expression creates a bilevel optimization structure. The reward depends on policy parameters $\theta$ both directly and indirectly through the implicit mapping $\theta \to \xi^\star(\theta)$. The subsequent policy gradient step treats $\xi^\star$ as fixed, ignoring the gradient path through the inner optimization loop. While a common computational shortcut, this introduces a known bias that is neither justified nor discussed, leaving the theoretical soundness of the combined discrete/continuous optimization underspecified.

comments.title: Overstated characterization of PQT's deterministic reward assumption
comments.quote: In contrast, PQT assumes deterministic rewards.
comments.explanation: The authors state that PQT 'assumes deterministic rewards' to motivate DSR's robustness. While PQT is theoretically grounded in deterministic MDPs and degrades with stochastic rewards, its algorithmic formulation does not strictly require deterministic rewards; it can be applied to stochastic environments (lacking the same guarantees). Framing this sensitivity as a hard assumption slightly misrepresents the baseline literature. The empirical contrast remains valid, but the phrasing should be softened to 'relies on deterministic rewards for theoretical guarantees'.

comments.title: Terminology conflation between 'best-case' and top-ε conditional mean
comments.quote: the best-case performance of the standard policy gradient plateaus early in training (Figure 2E, dashed blue curve), whereas the risk-seeking policy gradient continues to increase until the end of training (Figure 2E, dashed black curve).
comments.explanation: The narrative refers to the dashed curve as 'best-case performance', while the caption defines it as the 'mean reward of top ε fraction of the batch'. In standard terminology, 'best-case' implies the absolute maximum reward (tracked by the solid curve). Although the risk-seeking objective optimizes the top-$\varepsilon$ conditional mean, equating it directly with 'best-case performance' in the text could mislead readers. Adding a clarifying phrase (e.g., 'best-case performance, defined here as the top-ε conditional mean') would resolve the ambiguity.

comments.title: Inconsistent tree definition (binary vs. variable arity)
comments.quote: Expression trees are a type of binary tree in which internal nodes are mathematical operators and terminal nodes are input variables or constants. Operators may be unary (i.e. one argument, such as sine) or binary (i.e. two arguments, such as multiply).
comments.explanation: The authors define expression trees as a 'type of binary tree', which strictly implies every internal node has exactly two children. However, they immediately state that operators can be unary, meaning they only require one child. This is a technical contradiction unless the authors implicitly assume the use of dummy/placeholder children for unary operators, which is never mentioned. Later in the text, they note that 'a node’s value determines its number of children', which aligns with general (variable-arity) trees rather than strict binary trees.
